critique.summary: The article describes a DIY project where the author and a friend modify an apartment intercom system to integrate with Apple Home using a custom ESP32-based relay device. They bypass the original cellular intercom system by directly controlling the solenoid lock via a microcontroller, enabling remote gate unlocking through Apple Home.
mainPoints: The apartment intercom's cellular service was discontinued, rendering the voice call unlocking system inoperative.
Initial attempts to hack the Wi-Fi router and fake phone signals to control the gate failed due to system limitations and complexity.
The successful approach involved physically intercepting the solenoid control wire and installing an ESP32 relay board to control the gate lock.
The ESP32 device was programmed using Rust and the Matter protocol to integrate with Apple Home for remote control.
Power supply challenges were overcome by using an external battery and later a rectifying regulator to convert AC to DC power.
The final installation is discreet, does not interfere with the building's main access system, and allows remote unlocking of the gate via Apple Home.
critique.truthfulness: The technical details provided in the article align with known principles of hardware hacking and smart home integration. The Doorking intercom system and its cellular service discontinuation are plausible, and the approach of controlling a solenoid lock via a relay is a common hardware hacking method. The use of an ESP32 microcontroller and the Matter protocol for smart home integration is consistent with current technology trends. The challenges with power supply and Bluetooth/Wi-Fi stack conflicts on the ESP32 are also credible technical issues. However, the article is a personal project report without external sources or verification from the building management or the intercom manufacturer, so some claims about the system's configuration and vulnerabilities cannot be independently confirmed.
considerations: The legality and building management policies regarding modifying shared intercom systems are not discussed.
Potential security risks of bypassing the original intercom system and adding a custom relay device are not addressed.
Long-term reliability and maintenance of the DIY solution are not evaluated.
The article does not mention any consultation with professional locksmiths or security experts.
The impact on other residents or the building's overall security system is not considered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the challenges and creative solutions for integrating apartment intercom systems with modern smart home technology. Commenters share personal experiences, security concerns, and critiques of existing products and ecosystems.

comments.author: greggsy
comments.text:
I’m actually pretty surprised how bad the intercom ecosystem is these days.

Why aren’t there more ‘semi dumb’ Ethernet or wifi products that just let you announce that dinner is ready? It doesn’t need to be a fully ruggedised commercial system like this one or a fully integrated cloud managed solution like ring.

The cheap no name wireless ones can’t handle comms between rooms, let alone across a house.

The security implications aren’t insurmountable - you could use pairing codes if there are multiple on the network.

I’ve accepted that it’s a niche market, and that the only solution is to use Asterix with a some cheapo voip phones.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a reasonable critique of the current intercom market and highlights the niche nature of solutions, supported by practical considerations about security and technology.

comments.author: sharklasers123
comments.text: I did similar in my apartment in Amsterdam but a little more low-tech. I soldered the relay on an Esp8266 directly to the unlock button on the intercom PCB in my apartment. Worked flawlessly for years
comments.analysis: This comment shares a firsthand technical solution that aligns well with the article's theme, adding credibility through personal experience.

comments.author: anilakar
comments.text:
Frank's guests just need to get the Doorking 16120 default key and start letting themselves in.

Edit: undergrad shenanigans from ten years ago:

Our university student-run electronics lab had an issue: technically anyone with a student card was allowed on premises at any given time, but the department only gave us a small set of keys that we had to share with the rest of the student associations. Obviously we needed a solution.

We did some snooping and found that the request-to-exit button wire was running on a cable tray alongside all the other wiring and plumbing, as the lab was in the basement. We picked a suitably dark, inconspicuous spot and wired up a Raspberry Pi driving a transistor and in turn a relay which we then wired in parallel with that button. Users could then connect to the local lab wifi and then SSH into the device. Login shell was replaced with a script that pulsed the GPIO line for half a second and subsequently caused the door to open.

We never got caught and apparently all the evidence was destroyed when the building was renovated a few years later.
comments.analysis: This detailed anecdote illustrates a clever and practical hack to remotely control door access, relevant to the topic and plausible given the technical details.

comments.author: blitzar
comments.text:
I use a Ring Intercom Audio for a similar use. Works surprisingly well, I wish someone would clone the hardware and make an open version so Jeff didn't listen in every time someone rings my doorbell or buzz himself in whenever he wants.

No native apple home - homebridge handles that.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical example of a commercial product used for intercom purposes and raises valid privacy concerns about proprietary systems.

comments.author: jeffwilcox
comments.text:
Was always wanting to do something like this before they swapped ours out for a SaaS+hardware butterfly mx thing.

Those Doorkings have had to get replaced at so many buildings in Seattle now that criminals figured out how easy they were to override.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights real security vulnerabilities in common intercom systems, supporting the need for better solutions.

comments.author: sneak
comments.text:
Confessing to felonies, in writing, under one’s real name is wild.

Here’s hoping nobody decides to bother them about this. I’m not a lawyer but this appears to this layperson at the very least a CFAA violation by accessing the router and resetting its root password, as well as possibly criminal mischief as well as whatever stealing AC power is.

You couldn’t pay me to do a writeup like this, and I’d be wearing gloves the whole time.
comments.analysis: While raising important legal and ethical concerns about hacking intercom systems, the comment is speculative and lacks legal expertise, reducing its accuracy.

comments.author: cryptoz
comments.text:
Related, I'm still upset at the lies told by landlords regarding phone number privacy in buzz-in intercoms. I've been told multiple times at multiple apartment buildings, "don't worry, while the system will call your phone when someone taps your entry code, your phone number won't be revealed". And then you sign the lease, get a delivery from Instacart in your new place, and find that your 'private' number is blasted out loud, heard a whole city block away, in a loud-ass DTMF tone sequence.

BS.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration about privacy issues with intercom systems, which is plausible but anecdotal and less central to the technical discussion.

comments.author: dosinga
comments.text: I ended up using https://eu.switch-bot.com/products/switchbot-bot -- just have a finger robot push the button
comments.analysis: A simple mechanical workaround is suggested, which is practical but less sophisticated compared to other technical solutions discussed.

comments.author: FlorinSays
comments.text:
I had the same problem and I've searched for ready made solutions for over an year before I found a guy that reverse-engineers and builds ready-made boards to install in my intercom for less than 30 euro.

I'm unsure if I should post the link or not as it's specific to Romania, but I love how janky the buids are: https://www.olx.ro/d/oferta/automatizare-interfon-electra-cu...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a real-world example of a low-cost solution, though it is region-specific and less detailed.

comments.author: beardyw
comments.text: It's a little sad that, having realised that the simplest route for the hardware was the best, a simple route for communications wasn't explored. I suspect that cramming in a complex stack wasn't the best or quickest solution.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a thoughtful critique on design choices, emphasizing simplicity in communication protocols, which is a valid perspective.

comments.author: dwedge
comments.text:
Of course it had to mention Claude and Rust haha.

That aside, I enjoyed this read and it's such a niche thing that there is almost no way they'll step on the toes of another resident wanting to do the same thing
comments.analysis: This comment is mostly a lighthearted reaction with little substantive contribution to the technical discussion.

comments.author: wzdd
comments.text: Fun. I did this recently with mine. There's now a discreet USB cable running down from behind it...
comments.analysis: A brief personal note on a similar project, but lacks detail to assess accuracy or depth.
